name: Beira Interior
history: Beira Interior, located in the eastern interior of Portugal, has a long history shaped by its position on the frontier between Christian and Muslim territories during the medieval Reconquista. The region was gradually repopulated and fortified after the Christian conquest, with numerous castles and defensive structures built to protect against raids and maintain territorial control. During the Middle Ages and early modern period, Beira Interior developed as an agricultural and pastoral region, with towns like Covilhã becoming important centers for wool production and textile manufacturing. The region's development was often constrained by its inland location and distance from coastal trade routes, contributing to its more rural and traditional character.
culturalNotes: Beira Interior is known for its strong traditions in textile production, particularly wool weaving, which remains an important part of local identity and economy. The region maintains deep-rooted customs in rural life, traditional crafts, and folk celebrations that reflect its agricultural heritage and isolated mountain communities. The distinct regional cuisine features hearty mountain fare, game dishes, and locally produced cheeses and cured meats that showcase the area's pastoral traditions.
bestTimeToVisit: Best visited in spring or autumn to enjoy the mountainous landscape and avoid harsh winters.
languageNotes: The local dialect is a variation of Portuguese with distinctive phonetic characteristics and vocabulary influenced by the region's geographical isolation in the interior highlands. Visitors will find that standard Portuguese is widely understood, though locals may use regional expressions such as 'miúdo' for child or 'rapariga' in its traditional sense of young woman, which differ from coastal Portuguese usage.

places.name: Castelo Branco
places.description: The capital of Beira Interior region, Castelo Branco is renowned for its spectacular Jardim do Paço Episcopal, a baroque garden adorned with fountains and hedges. The city also features a medieval castle ruin, traditional embroidery arts, and serves as a cultural and administrative hub.

activities.detail: Wander through this magnificent baroque garden featuring ornamental fountains, precisely trimmed hedges, and stone staircases that exemplify 18th-century Portuguese landscaping.
Discover the remnants of the medieval fortress that once dominated the city, offering panoramic views of Castelo Branco and the surrounding Beira Interior landscape.

activities.text: Tour the Museu de Francisco Tavares Proença Júnior
activities.detail: Examine regional artifacts, archaeological finds, and traditional crafts that document the cultural heritage of Beira Interior.
Browse local artisan workshops and markets to purchase the city's famous white embroidery work, featuring intricate geometric patterns on linen and cotton.

activities.text: Stroll Through the Historic City Center
activities.detail: Walk the cobbled streets to appreciate Renaissance and baroque architecture, including the Igreja de São Miguel and traditional Portuguese townhouses.
Enjoy this modern urban green space with walking paths, benches, and views that provide respite from sightseeing in the heart of the city.

food.detail: A traditional corn cake with a dense, slightly sweet crumb that is a beloved local pastry often served with coffee or at celebrations.
A slow-braised goat or lamb stew cooked in red wine and spices, characteristic of Beira Interior mountain cuisine and rich comfort food.
Fresh grilled or pan-fried trout sourced from the region's clear mountain streams, typically seasoned simply with olive oil and lemon.
A creamy, soft sheep's cheese from the nearby Serra da Estrela mountains, with a distinctive tangy flavor best enjoyed with fresh bread.
A custard-like dessert made with eggs, milk, and cinnamon baked until golden, reflecting medieval Portuguese sweet traditions.

culture.detail: The intricate white embroidery (bordados) remains an important cultural practice; visitors should respect workshops as active artisan spaces and purchase directly to support local artisans.
Many religious sites remain active places of worship; dress modestly and avoid visiting during services unless you wish to participate respectfully.

culture.text: Spring and autumn are ideal seasons
culture.detail: Castelo Branco experiences cold winters and warm summers, so visiting in May-June or September-October offers comfortable weather for exploring gardens and castle ruins.
While some tourism professionals speak English, learning basic Portuguese phrases enhances interaction with locals and shows respect for the community.

places.name: Guarda
places.description: Portugal's highest city, perched at 1,056 meters, Guarda commands dramatic views across the Iberian landscape and features a stunning Gothic cathedral and well-preserved medieval walls. The city offers excellent hiking opportunities, traditional cuisine, and serves as the gateway to the Serra da Estrela National Park.

activities.detail: Explore this magnificent Gothic cathedral with its intricate stone carvings and soaring interior, built over two centuries starting in the 14th century.
Stroll along the remarkably preserved defensive walls that encircle the old town, offering panoramic views across three countries.

activities.text: Serra da Estrela National Park Hiking
activities.detail: Access Portugal's highest mountain range just 30 kilometers away, with trails ranging from easy valley walks to challenging alpine routes.
Visit this regional museum housed in a 16th-century palace to discover archaeological finds, religious art, and local historical artifacts.
Climb this restored medieval tower for sweeping views of the surrounding landscape and the city's layout from above.
Relax in the main plaza surrounded by elegant buildings, local cafés, and the atmospheric heart of Guarda's social life.

food.detail: This traditional cornmeal cake is a local specialty with a dense, slightly sweet texture often served with coffee in local bakeries.
Sample this creamy, legendary sheep's cheese from nearby mountains, best eaten fresh with a simple spoon and rustic bread.
Tender pork cooked in red wine with local herbs and vegetables, representing the hearty cuisine of the interior highlands.
Dark rye bread baked locally using traditional methods, perfect for pairing with cured meats and Serra cheese at any meal.

culture.detail: Guarda's high altitude means winters are cold and many summer visitors depart, offering a more authentic and peaceful experience.
This is a working city, not a tourist resort; locals value quietness and tradition, so embrace slower rhythms and shop during posted hours.
English is less common here than coastal areas, so greeting shopkeepers and locals with 'Olá' and 'Obrigado' opens doors and shows respect.

places.name: Serra da Estrela
places.description: Portugal's highest mountain range reaching 1,993 meters, Serra da Estrela is a protected nature reserve offering alpine scenery, hiking trails, and the charming village of Manteigas with natural hot springs. The region is essential for experiencing pristine mountain landscapes, wildlife, and traditional shepherd culture.

activities.detail: Climb to Portugal's highest peak at 1,993 meters for panoramic views of the entire mountain range and surrounding plains.
Soak in the warm thermal waters of Caldas de Manteigas, naturally heated springs located in the charming village of Manteigas.
Follow the glacial valley carved by the Zêzere River with stunning waterfalls, alpine meadows, and traditional shepherd settlements.

activities.text: Covão da Ametade Glacial Lake Walk
activities.detail: Explore one of Portugal's rare glacial lakes surrounded by stark alpine terrain and ancient stone shepherd huts.
Wander the narrow cobblestone streets of this 18th-century mountain village with traditional granite architecture and local artisan shops.
Trek through protected meadows in spring and summer to observe rare Iberian wolves, eagles, and mountain wildflowers in their natural habitat.
Follow the dramatic ridge trail offering sheer cliff views and access to remote shepherd routes used for centuries.

food.detail: This creamy, award-winning sheep cheese from the region has a distinctive flavor and is best eaten fresh with mountain bread.
A traditional warming goat stew with wild herbs and potatoes that shepherds have eaten for generations during mountain seasons.
Fresh-caught trout from the Zêzere and other alpine streams, grilled simply with local herbs and served in village restaurants.
Dense rye bread baked in wood ovens using traditional methods, perfect for pairing with local cheese and cured meats.
Wild mountain honey collected from alpine meadows, particularly prized for its floral complexity and use in traditional remedies.
Sample wines from the nearby Beira Interior region, particularly Touriga Nacional, at local mountain lodges and village restaurants.

culture.detail: The region's identity centers on transhumance shepherding; visitors should greet herders respectfully if encountered on trails and understand this ancient practice remains vital to local culture.
Weather changes rapidly above 1,500 meters year-round; always carry layers and rain gear even on clear mornings, as afternoon storms are common.
Small mountain villages close many shops and restaurants between 13:00 and 15:00 for lunch; plan accordingly and embrace the local rhythm rather than expect constant service.

While some English is spoken in tourist areas, learning basic Portuguese phrases shows respect and significantly enhances interactions with elderly villagers and mountain guides who maintain traditional knowledge.

places.name: Manteigas
places.description: A picturesque mountain village in the heart of Serra da Estrela, Manteigas is famous for its natural hot springs and the dramatic Vale da Zêzere gorge nearby. It's a perfect base for mountain walks, enjoying local sheep cheese, and experiencing authentic mountain village life.

activities.detail: Trek through the dramatic granite gorge carved by the Zêzere River, featuring steep cliffs, natural pools, and stunning viewpoints.
Relax in the natural thermal spa complex fed by warm mineral waters that have been used for healing since the 18th century.
Climb to the highest point in continental Portugal at Torre (1,993m) for panoramic views across the Serra da Estrela mountain range.
Wander the narrow cobblestone streets of this authentic mountain village to discover traditional stone houses and local craft shops.
Visit this refreshing natural swimming hole in the Zêzere River, perfect for cooling off after mountain walks.
Explore the Ice Age landscape with its U-shaped valleys, alpine meadows, and unique flora found only at high altitude in Serra da Estrela.

food.detail: A creamy, semi-soft sheep's cheese made from milk of ewes grazing in the mountains, best enjoyed with fresh bread at local restaurants.
Grilled lamb kebab skewered on bay laurel branches, a traditional mountain dish cooked over open fires in village restaurants.
A hearty goat broth flavored with mountain herbs and vegetables, warming comfort food typical of Serra da Estrela cuisine.
Dense, dark rye bread baked locally in traditional wood-fired ovens, a staple accompaniment to every meal in Manteigas.
Mountain honey produced from wildflowers growing at high altitude, sold at local markets with distinctive flavor and medicinal properties.

culture.detail: Weather in Serra da Estrela changes rapidly; locals advise bringing layers and checking conditions before any hike, as fog and cold descend quickly.
In this tight-knit village community, a simple 'Olá' or 'Bom dia' when entering shops or passing on streets is appreciated and expected.
Local produce and cheese markets operate primarily in morning hours; arrive early to find the best selections of fresh mountain goods.
Many residents craft traditional goods like wool blankets and wooden items; purchasing directly supports the village economy and preserves mountain heritage.

places.name: Monsanto
places.description: One of Portugal's most distinctive villages, Monsanto features buildings ingeniously built between and around massive granite boulders that dominate the landscape. The village exudes medieval charm and has earned recognition as one of Europe's most beautiful villages due to its unique architecture and dramatic hilltop setting.

activities.detail: Climb to the summit of Monsanto's hilltop to discover the remains of the medieval castle with panoramic views across the Beira Interior landscape.
Wander through the maze of cobblestone pathways that wind between and beneath massive granite boulders, experiencing the village's unique medieval architecture firsthand.

activities.text: Visit the Chapel of São Miguel
activities.detail: Discover this small whitewashed chapel perched among the rocks, offering intimate spiritual atmosphere and stunning views from its elevated position.
Position yourself among the boulders during golden hour to capture the dramatic interplay of light and shadow on Monsanto's distinctive granite formations and traditional buildings.
Trek the paths radiating outward from the village through pine forests and scrubland to experience the broader natural beauty of the Serra da Estrela foothills.
Examine how residents ingeniously integrated their homes into and around the enormous boulders, with roofs supported by granite and walls built directly against stone faces.

food.detail: A traditional dense cornbread from the region, often served at local cafés and bakeries, that pairs perfectly with local cheese or jam.
A creamy, tangy sheep's milk cheese from the Serra da Estrela region that represents the pastoral heritage of Beira Interior's dairy traditions.
A rustic hearty broth featuring beans, vegetables, and sometimes meat, traditionally cooked over open fires and emblematic of Monsanto's humble, resourceful culinary heritage.

food.text: Pão de Milho com Broa (Mixed Grain Bread)
food.detail: Local bakeries produce this hearty rye and corn blend that reflects centuries of grain cultivation in the region's terraced farmlands.
Sample the golden honey harvested from hives positioned among the wildflowers and herbs covering the surrounding hillsides, sold at village shops.

culture.text: Respect Private Spaces Among the Rocks
culture.detail: Remember that many boulder-integrated structures are active homes with residents; stay on public pathways and avoid peering into windows or photographing people without permission.

culture.text: Visit During Spring or Early Fall
culture.detail: The village experiences extreme summer heat trapped among the boulders and can be snow-covered in winter; the mild shoulder seasons offer the most comfortable exploration experience.
While some business owners speak English, most residents speak only Portuguese, and a simple 'Olá' or 'Bom dia' demonstrates respect for local culture and enhances interactions.
The uneven granite surfaces and steep cobblestone streets require good ankle support and non-slip soles; flip-flops and smooth-soled shoes are impractical and potentially unsafe.

places.name: Sortelha
places.description: A perfectly preserved medieval walled village with narrow cobblestone streets and ancient fortifications, Sortelha appears frozen in time with traditional stone houses and castle ruins. The village offers an authentic glimpse into medieval rural Portugal and stunning views across the surrounding countryside.

activities.detail: Traverse the intact 13th-century fortification walls that encircle the village, offering panoramic views of the Serra da Gardunha mountains and surrounding countryside.
Climb to the hilltop ruins of Sortelha's medieval castle to see the remaining stone foundations and towers that once dominated this strategic frontier settlement.
Get lost in the narrow, winding stone alleyways lined with traditional granite houses, many featuring wooden doors and windows that have remained unchanged for centuries.
Step inside the modest stone church at the village center to see its simple Romanesque architecture and understand local religious life across the centuries.
Capture the golden light illuminating the stone fortifications and village rooftops from the western viewpoint as the sun sets over the Serra da Gardunha.

food.detail: A traditional regional dessert made with cornmeal, honey, and cinnamon that reflects the agricultural heritage of Beira Interior and is often served at local bakeries.
Slow-roasted kid goat seasoned with garlic and herbs, a classic mountain dish reflecting the pastoral traditions of the Serra da Gardunha region.

food.text: Queijo da Serra da Gardunha (Mountain Cheese)
food.detail: A creamy, semi-soft sheep and goat cheese produced locally in the surrounding highlands, known for its rich flavor and traditional production methods.
Despite inland location, this traditional Portuguese soup appears in regional menus, made with dried shark and chickpeas, representing historical trade routes and cultural exchange.

culture.detail: Visit in early morning or late afternoon to experience the village with fewer tourists and see locals going about their daily routines in this authentic settlement.
Many houses in Sortelha remain as private homes; photograph respectfully and avoid entering courtyards or gardens without explicit permission from residents.
The village's steep cobblestone streets and uneven medieval pathways require sturdy footwear; casual strolling shoes will quickly become uncomfortable.

places.name: Idanha-a-Velha
places.description: A haunting archaeological site and virtually abandoned village, Idanha-a-Velha preserves remnants of Roman, Visigothic, and medieval periods within its ancient stone walls. The site is fascinating for history enthusiasts and offers an atmospheric glimpse into centuries of Iberian heritage.

activities.detail: Walk through the remains of the early Christian cathedral, one of the oldest religious structures in the Iberian Peninsula, dating back to the 6th century.
Examine artifacts and inscribed stones from the Visigothic period, including funerary markers and decorative fragments housed in a small museum within the village.
Trace the remains of Roman and medieval defensive walls that encircle the village, offering views across the Tagus valley and glimpses into centuries of military architecture.
Observe carved Latin inscriptions and sculptural fragments scattered throughout the village, evidence of Idanha's importance as a Roman settlement.

activities.text: Photograph the Bridge over the Ponsul River
activities.detail: Document the Roman bridge that still spans the Ponsul River, a striking engineering feat that has survived millennia of weathering.
Stroll through narrow cobblestone lanes bordered by crumbling stone houses, experiencing the haunting atmosphere that defines this ghostly village.

food.detail: Sample the dense, rustic sourdough bread made with local grains, a staple that has sustained the region for centuries and reflects the harsh mountain terroir.
Try this traditional stewed goat or lamb cooked slowly in red wine with local herbs, a dish that embodies Beira Interior's pastoral heritage.
Taste the creamy, mild sheep's cheese from the nearby Serra da Estrela mountains, which can be eaten soft with a spoon or firmer when aged.
Enjoy freshly caught river trout prepared simply with olive oil and herbs, reflecting the area's clear mountain waters and traditional fishing practices.
Sample this hearty peasant dish of bread crumbs fried with garlic, pork, and regional greens, a warming comfort food typical of interior Portugal.

culture.detail: Walk quietly and respectfully through the ancient cathedral and burial areas, as this is an active archaeological and historical site with deep spiritual significance to Portuguese heritage.
The village offers minimal shade and few services; visitors should arrive well-prepared with water, sturdy walking shoes, and sun protection for extended exploration.
Spring and autumn provide the most comfortable conditions and allow for a more contemplative experience without summer crowds or winter dampness affecting the atmospheric quality.
Seek out information from local guides or the small museum staff, whose deep knowledge of the site's layers enriches understanding of Roman, Visigothic, and medieval periods.

places.name: Pinhel
places.description: A charming walled town with impressive medieval fortifications and a strategic position on the border, Pinhel features well-preserved ramparts and traditional architecture. The town offers excellent walking paths along its walls and serves as a quiet base for exploring the northern Beira Interior region.

activities.detail: Explore the fully preserved 13th-century fortification walls that encircle the town, offering panoramic views of the surrounding countryside and the Côa River valley.
Climb to the remains of Pinhel Castle at the town's highest point to discover archaeological remnants and enjoy sweeping vistas across the Beira Interior landscape.
The Museu de Pinhel displays artifacts from Roman times through the medieval period, documenting the town's strategic importance as a border fortress.
Trek along marked trails connecting Pinhel to surrounding rural hamlets like Sortelha and Belmonte, passing through cork forests and traditional agricultural lands.
The Igreja Matriz features Renaissance-era architecture and decorative tiles, representing the religious heart of this historic community.
Pinhel's position near the Spanish border offers dramatic vistas and photo opportunities across the deeply carved valleys that have shaped the region for centuries.

food.detail: A traditional cornmeal cake from Beira Interior, often served at local bakeries with a dense, slightly sweet texture and sometimes studded with pine nuts.
Roasted young goat prepared with garlic and herbs, a celebrated regional specialty that showcases the local pastoral tradition and is served at family-run restaurants.
Sample the creamy, renowned cheese from the nearby Serra da Estrela mountains, available at local markets and often paired with regional bread and wine.
A hearty bread-based soup thickened with eggs and garlic, reflecting the rustic culinary heritage shared across southern Beira Interior.
Fresh-caught trout from the Côa River and tributary streams, typically grilled simply with olive oil and served at traditional riverside eateries.

culture.detail: Pinhel's residents maintain strong ties to their Spanish neighbors across the border, and you'll notice a blend of Portuguese and Castilian cultural influences in daily life.
The town's weekly market remains a vital social gathering; visiting on market day lets you experience authentic local commerce and community interaction.
In this quiet border region, locals appreciate visitors who acknowledge their history and speak slowly; small gestures of interest in the town's medieval past are warmly received.
The compact walled town is best experienced on foot, and locals expect visitors to navigate the steep, narrow cobblestone streets slowly and respectfully.

places.name: Sabugal
places.description: A historic border town dominated by its distinctive five-towered castle with unusual pentagonal design, Sabugal offers medieval charm and strategic importance in Portuguese-Spanish history. The castle and surrounding old town are rewarding to explore for architecture and regional heritage.

activities.detail: Climb the five distinctive towers of this 13th-century pentagonal fortress to enjoy panoramic views of the border region and understand its strategic medieval importance.
Stroll through narrow cobbled streets lined with traditional stone houses, granite doorways, and period architecture that reflect centuries of Portuguese-Spanish border culture.

activities.text: Visit the Igreja de Santa Maria do Pinheiro
activities.detail: This 16th-century church features Manueline architectural elements and houses religious art representative of Beira Interior's heritage.
Walk the nearby trails to the Spanish frontier to experience the borderland landscape that defined Sabugal's historical significance.
Examine local archaeological finds, medieval artifacts, and exhibits documenting Sabugal's role as a fortified border settlement.

food.detail: Fresh trout caught from the nearby Côa River, often prepared simply grilled with local olive oil and herbs, is a regional specialty.
Slow-roasted lamb, seasoned with regional herbs and garlic, reflects the pastoral traditions of Beira Interior's upland communities.
A traditional cornmeal cake, sometimes with honey or nuts, that is a common harvest-time treat throughout the region.
Creamy, tangy sheep's cheese from the nearby Serra da Estrela mountains, often served with local bread at restaurants and markets.
A hearty kale and potato soup made with regional ingredients, offering warming comfort typical of interior Portuguese cuisine.

culture.detail: Sabugal's identity is deeply tied to centuries of Portuguese-Spanish military and cultural tension; locals appreciate visitors who show genuine interest in this layered history.
In this traditional border town, a polite 'Olá' or 'Bom dia' goes a long way, as residents value courtesy and respectful engagement.
Check for Sabugal's medieval fair or religious processions to experience authentic community traditions and regional celebrations.
The castle and old town are best experienced by walking the steep, narrow streets where cars cannot easily go, revealing hidden details and local life.

places.name: Penamacor
places.description: An ancient border town perched dramatically on a rocky hilltop with remnants of medieval fortifications, Penamacor offers panoramic views and an off-the-beaten-path authentic experience. The town preserves traditional stone architecture and provides excellent access to the unspoiled eastern Beira Interior landscape.

activities.detail: Wander through the remains of Penamacor's medieval castle perched on the hilltop, offering sweeping views across the Beira Interior landscape and insight into the town's strategic border defensive role.
Explore this 16th-century parish church featuring traditional Portuguese Manueline architecture and azulejo tiles in the town center.
Stroll through the narrow, winding cobblestone streets lined with traditional granite and schist houses that characterize the authentic medieval town layout.
Hike to this dramatic rocky outcrop near town for panoramic vistas of the eastern Beira Interior's unspoiled valleys and forest landscape.
Visit this small museum housed in a traditional building showcasing local archaeological finds, medieval artifacts, and ethnographic displays related to border town life.
Explore the forested mountain trails surrounding Penamacor offering access to pristine natural countryside and traditional rural hamlets.

food.text: Bife de Novilho à Moda da Casa
food.detail: A hearty grilled beef steak prepared simply with local herbs and olive oil, representing the region's pastoral cattle-raising tradition and served in family-run restaurants.
A traditional pumpkin and bread soup typical of Beira Interior, often enriched with local olive oil and served as a comforting first course during cooler months.
A creamy, protected-designation sheep's cheese from the nearby Serra da Estrela mountains, available at local markets and paired with regional bread.
A risotto-style rice dish with rabbit, a traditional Beira Interior specialty reflecting the region's hunting heritage and local game availability.
Dense cornbread cakes made with local corn, traditionally eaten with cheese or cured meats for breakfast or as a travel snack through rural Portugal.
Homemade cured sausages and charcuterie prepared by local families using traditional recipes, available at markets or directly from producers in surrounding villages.

culture.text: Greet with Respect in Small Spaces
culture.detail: In this tight-knit border community, a simple 'Olá' or 'Bom dia' when entering small shops or streets is expected and greatly appreciated by locals.
Penamacor celebrates its medieval heritage during summer festivals featuring traditional music, crafts, and food; timing your visit around these events provides deeper cultural immersion.
As an off-the-beaten-path rural town, services operate at a leisurely pace and some shops may close during lunch hours; plan accordingly and embrace the slower rhythm.
Many residents still practice traditional crafts like stone masonry, weaving, and cheese-making; purchasing directly from makers supports the community and provides authentic souvenirs.

restaurants.name: Solares do Convento
restaurants.address: Rua Marquês de Pombal 12, Castelo Branco
restaurants.description: Elegant dining in a converted convent with traditional Beira Interior recipes and refined presentation.

restaurants.name: O Recanto
restaurants.description: Cosy restaurant specialising in hearty mountain dishes and local cheese and cured meat platters.

restaurants.name: Casa da Calçada
restaurants.description: Historic stone-walled restaurant in the fortified city serving elevated traditional cuisine with local ingredients.

restaurants.name: Tasco do Jaime
restaurants.description: Family-run tavern with simple, authentic mountain cooking and generous portions of traditional fare.

restaurants.name: Aldeias da Rocha
restaurants.description: Rural restaurant near the border villages offering authentic Beira recipes and game dishes in a rustic setting.

restaurants.name: A Casa do Forno
restaurants.description: Intimate village restaurant in the Serra da Estrela foothills serving seasonal mountain produce and local specials.

restaurants.name: Restaurante Dom Sancho
restaurants.description: Modern take on regional cuisine in the medieval city with carefully sourced local products and creative plating.

restaurants.name: O Espelho
restaurants.description: Bright, welcoming restaurant featuring slow-cooked regional specialities and a strong wine list from Beira vineyards.

restaurants.name: Taberna da Esquina
restaurants.description: Unpretentious corner tavern in a walled border town serving honest home cooking and local wine by the jug.

restaurants.name: Restaurante Abrigo da Montanha
restaurants.description: Casual mountain lodge atmosphere featuring warming stews, roasted meats, and views towards Serra da Estrela.

restaurants.name: Casa Museu
restaurants.description: Restaurant in a historic manor house combining museum atmosphere with well-executed traditional border region cooking.

restaurants.name: Adega do Célio
restaurants.description: Wine-focused adega in the historic centre pairing local varieties with simple, quality regional charcuterie and cheeses.

restaurants.name: O Meu Recanto
restaurants.description: Country restaurant near the historic castle serving traditional Beira dishes in a warm, family-friendly environment.

restaurants.name: Restaurante Porta da Vila
restaurants.address: Rua da Porta da Vila 7, Sortelha
restaurants.description: Medieval village setting inside walled Sortelha offering refined takes on local game and mountain recipes.

restaurants.name: Café Restaurante Central
restaurants.description: Central meeting place in the southern Beira town serving unpretentious daily specials and regional comfort food.
